one_line_takeaway: SeqWM watermarks LLM-agent trajectories by conditioning the keyed bias on a sliding window of recent actions rather than absolute step index, giving deletion-robust, position-agnostic detection with a finite-sample-valid random-key-calibrated p-value that reliably separates watermarked from unwatermarked trajectories (z up to ~11.4, p<=0.01 in 9/9 model-benchmark combinations) where round-indexed and payload-based baselines fail.
key_finding: Table 2 (main detection results, z-score / p-value / per-step hit rate across ToolBench, ALFWorld, OASIS and all three backbones): SeqWM is the only method that reliably rejects the null in every one of the 9 model-benchmark combinations, with p=0.001 in 8 of 9 cells and p=0.010 in the ninth (Qwen on OASIS, z=2.53). Representative z-scores for SeqWM: Gemma/ToolBench z=7.77, Llama/ToolBench z=9.22, Qwen/ToolBench z=5.48; Gemma/ALFWorld z=9.45, Llama/ALFWorld z=10.18, Qwen/ALFWorld z=11.44; Gemma/OASIS z=4.67, Llama/OASIS z=5.16, Qwen/OASIS z=2.53. Corresponding per-step hit rates range from 63% to 100% (e.g., Llama/OASIS hit rate 100%). By contrast, AgentGuide never reaches significance at alpha=0.01 in any cell (best case z=1.83, p=0.032 on Gemma/ToolBench) and is sometimes anti-correlated with the watermark hypothesis (e.g., Llama/ToolBench z=-0.54); AgentMark, evaluated on the same single-bit metrics though this is not its native task, likewise never separates from the null (p ranges 0.087-0.981) and unwatermarked sequences show z near zero as expected. Table 1 (mean-shift validation) confirms the first-order SNR prediction: measured score gap Delta-S vs. predicted Delta-S_pred matches within a small constant factor across all three domains (ToolBench: predicted 193.0 vs. measured 216.8, ratio 1.12; ALFWorld: predicted 348.5 vs. measured 318.1, ratio 0.91; OASIS: predicted 43.8 vs. measured 62.4, ratio 1.42), and the random-key null estimator is unbiased, with the measured gap at most 1.74% of E[S] under unwatermarked sequences across all domains. The gamma-sensitivity sweep on OASIS (gamma in {0.5,...,3.0}, m=8 fixed) shows the multi-channel detector reaches p<0.01 from gamma=2.0 onward on all three backbones, while a single-channel (m=1) ablation stays near the noise floor even at gamma=3.0, demonstrating that redundant multi-channel evidence, not bias strength alone, drives reliable detection. Under the deletion-rate sweep (Figure 3), AgentGuide collapses at small deletion rates because a single missing action misaligns all downstream round-indexed subsets, the single-channel SeqWM variant avoids catastrophic misalignment but has too thin a score margin to survive substantial corruption, while the full multi-channel SeqWM detector's TPR degrades gradually rather than catastrophically across all three domains as deletion rate rises to 50%.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is SeqWM, a behavioral watermarking framework for LLM agents built from three components: (1) a history-conditioned multi-channel encoder that seeds the keyed guided-subset via a sliding window of the w preceding actions (ct = (b_{t-w},...,b_{t-1})) rather than the absolute round index used by prior work (AgentGuide), replicated across m independent channels via an appended channel index, with an exponential-tilt bias P^wm_t(b) = (1/Z_t) P_t(b) exp(gamma * s_t(b)/m); (2) a sliding-window detector that aggregates evidence from every length-w substring of an observed trajectory without needing absolute positional alignment between encoder and decoder (Eq. 9); and (3) a random-key calibration procedure that builds an empirical null distribution by re-scoring the same observed sequence under M wrong keys, yielding a finite-sample-valid p-value (Eq. 12) without assuming independence among detector indicators. The paper also proves a deletion-robustness theorem (Theorem 4.1) showing round-indexed detectors suffer an O(1/rho) expected score gap after a first deletion at fraction rho (vanishing relative to the clean-sequence Theta(T) gap as T grows), while the sliding-window score obeys a deterministic bound S(K, b-hat) >= S(K, b_1:T) - d(w+1)m, i.e. each deleted position invalidates at most (w+1)m indicators. Empirically, SeqWM is evaluated against two prior behavioral watermarks (AgentGuide, AgentMark) and an unwatermarked baseline.
threat_model: The detector observes an action sequence b-hat_1:T' (possibly a corrupted/deleted/truncated/partially-observed subsequence of the true trajectory) together with a secret key K, and must decide whether it was produced by a watermarked agent. Corruption is modeled primarily as deletion of arbitrary positions chosen uniformly at random (T' <= T). The motivating scenarios given in the introduction are: verifying which agent produced an observed trajectory to detect impersonation/coordinated manipulation, attributing automated content to its originating system, defending proprietary agents against imitation/model-extraction attacks where an adversary harvests trajectories via API access and trains a surrogate, and protecting trajectory datasets from unattributed downstream reuse. The paper explicitly frames this as a provenance/IP-protection problem, not an attack-detection-on-content problem; it is a defensive watermarking scheme, and its own stated limitation (Appendix A) is that it does not yet engage adaptive adversaries or semantic-level transformations such as paraphrasing/action substitution.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Appendix A, Conclusion): the construction has not been tested against stronger adaptive adversaries or semantic-level transformations such as paraphrasing or semantically-equivalent action substitutions -- the paper explicitly lists this as an open direction rather than a solved case, and notes the redundant-coding perspective is only expected, not shown, to help there. Analytically, the paper itself shows that for a plain sum-score detector, the ratio SNR^2/DKL is independent of the channel count m -- i.e., multi-channel redundancy is not an information-theoretic detection-power gain, only a robustness/calibration/extensibility benefit -- and the authors state this plainly to avoid overclaiming what redundancy buys. The deletion-robustness bound in Theorem 4.1(b) is an additive worst-case bound on invalidated indicators, not a direct guarantee on post-deletion detection power/TPR (the paper is explicit that empirical TPR results in Section 5 are needed to characterize actual post-attack detection). All experiments use small/mid-size open-weight backbones (3B-4B parameters) and moderate-scale benchmarks (200, 50, and 100 episodes/steps respectively); no evaluation against production-scale or closed-weight commercial agents (e.g., the paper's own motivating examples -- Claude Code, Deep Research, Copilot) is reported. Utility preservation is asserted ("preserving agent utility") but the excerpted text does not surface a quantified task-success-rate utility metric in the main tables reviewed here.

datasets_used: ToolBench (200 queries under a ReAct loop, max 6 steps/episode; 50-query subset used for the gamma-sensitivity sweep)
ALFWorld (50 tasks, max episode length 50, one-shot prompting)
OASIS Reddit (100 simulation steps, 1 user agent + 3 NPC agents, persona tech_geek)
quantitative_results: Table 2 detection z-scores for SeqWM: Gemma ToolBench z=7.77 p=0.001 Hit%=67%; Llama ToolBench z=9.22 p=0.001 Hit%=81%; Qwen ToolBench z=5.48 p=0.001 Hit%=63%; Gemma ALFWorld z=9.45 p=0.001 Hit%=66%; Llama ALFWorld z=10.18 p=0.001 Hit%=75%; Qwen ALFWorld z=11.44 p=0.001 Hit%=79%; Gemma OASIS z=4.67 p=0.001 Hit%=99%; Llama OASIS z=5.16 p=0.001 Hit%=100%; Qwen OASIS z=2.53 p=0.010 Hit%=97%
AgentGuide baseline best case: Gemma ToolBench z=1.83 p=0.032 Hit%=23% (never reaches p<0.01 anywhere); e.g. Qwen ALFWorld z=-2.17 p=0.985
AgentMark baseline (evaluated on non-native single-bit metric): p ranges from 0.087 to 0.981 across all cells, never significant at alpha=0.01

Table 1 mean-shift validation: ToolBench predicted Delta-S=193.0 vs measured 216.8 (ratio 1.12); ALFWorld predicted 348.5 vs measured 318.1 (ratio 0.91); OASIS predicted 43.8 vs measured 62.4 (ratio 1.42); null-sequence measured gap <=1.74% of E[S] across domains
gamma-sensitivity sweep on OASIS (m=8 fixed): multi-channel detector reaches p<0.01 from gamma=2.0 onward on all three backbones; single-channel ablation stays near noise floor even at gamma=3.0
Default hyperparameters: window w=3, channels m=8, guided-subset size n=3 (n=2 on OASIS), floor delta=0.2, gamma=2.0, alpha=0.01, M=1000 calibration trials

Deletion-rate robustness sweep tested at r in {0,5,10,20,30,50}%; AgentGuide collapses at small deletion rates while full SeqWM degrades gradually across all three domains (Figure 3, qualitative description in text; no single scalar TPR values given in the excerpted body text)

Compute: main rollouts on 2x NVIDIA A100 GPUs (SLURM suma_a100 partition), sanity checks/analysis on 2x NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell GPUs (suma_pro6000); a full 200-query ToolBench run across all modes takes roughly 4-6 hours of A100 wall time per agent LLM
baselines_compared: AgentGuide (Huang et al., round-indexed behavioral watermark that biases marginal per-step action frequency toward a keyed subset, detected via one-sided z-test against a binomial null)
AgentMark (Huang et al., multi-bit payload watermark preserving elicited per-step marginal distribution; evaluated here on non-native single-bit z/p metrics for cross-method comparability)
Single-channel (m=1) ablation of SeqWM's own multi-channel detector
